title-group.article-title: IL-37/IL-1R8 axis: a novel major mechanism of control at the interface between tumor and immune cells

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: Interleukin (IL)-37 is one of the “youngest” IL-1 family members and one of the few molecules exerting anti-inflammatory activity. Upon inflammasome activation, the cytokine precursor is converted into its mature form, which acts intracellularly as a nuclear transcription factor, impairing the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines, and extracellularly by forming the IL-37/IL-18Rα/IL-1R8 complex, favoring IL-1R8 inhibitory signaling with immunosuppressive function. IL-1R8, which is mostly expressed in a number of cell types, negatively regulates both IL-1R/TLR signaling, blocking the NF-kB/JNK pathway and the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines. Owing to its ability to inhibit both innate and adaptive immunity, IL-37 has been reported to control inflammation in many chronic disorders, including cancer. IL-37 impairs the proliferation and migration of tumor cells, mediates anti-angiogenetic mechanisms, and favors immunoregulation in the tumor microenvironment (TME). This review aims to provide a current overview of IL-37 genetic and biological features and of its active interaction with IL-1R8, inducing anti-inflammatory effects on the immune system and affecting cancer cell dynamics in the TME. Moreover, it analyzes the rare pro-tumoral effects of IL-37 in some tumors and discusses their possible mechanisms. It concludes that, due to its strong anti-inflammatory property, IL-37 can be considered a potential regulator in the pathogenesis of a variety of cancers, slowing tumor progression through multiple pathways and providing valuable information for tumor immune target therapy.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The interleukin (IL)-1 superfamily includes a number of molecules broadly distributed in all vertebrates and having pleiotropic actions in cell homeostasis, defense, inflammation, and cancer. Among its members, there are receptor chains and soluble ligands with agonistic, antagonistic, and pro- or anti-inflammatory properties.1

The IL-1 receptor family (IL-1Rs) comprises ten structurally related receptor molecules (named IL1R1-10 according to2-4 and the IL-18 binding protein (IL-18BP)) (Figure 1). Within the family, there are ligand-binding signaling receptors (i.e. IL-1R1 or CD121a, IL-1R4 or ST2, IL-1R5 or IL-18 receptor α chain [IL-18Rα]), accessory chains (i.e IL-1R3 or IL-1RAcP, IL-1R7 or IL-18 receptor β chain [IL-18Rβ]), decoy or signaling inhibitors (i.e IL-18 BP- and IL-1R8), and molecules such as IL-1R9 and IL-1R10 with no signaling capabilities following ligand engagement.3,4 IL-1Rs are formed by two chains: each chain typically displays three extracellular Ig-like domains (except IL-1R8 and IL-18BP, which contain only one Ig-like domain), a transmembrane- and an intracellular Toll-IL-1-receptor (TIR) signaling domain (Figure 1). The latter shares the ability with Toll-like receptors (TLRs) to recognize harmfull damage- and pathogen-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs and PAMPs).2

caption.p: IL-1 family ligands and receptors. The IL-1 family includes four (IL-1-, IL-18-, IL-36- and unknown-) ligand subfamilies, nine ligands (black), ten receptors (blue), eight receptor antagonists (pink), and one structurally related binding protein (orange). The receptors are formed by two chains, each containing three immunoglobulin (Ig)-like domains (IL-1R8 has only one Ig domain) and an intracellular Toll-IL-1-receptor (TIR) domain (IL-1R8 TIR domain has two amino acid substitutions); the receptor engagement may exert pro- and anti-inflammatory signals.

sec.p: Following ligand recognition, IL-1Rs dimerize and recruit the adaptor protein myeloid differentiation primary response 88 (MyD88) (as part of a highly conserved signaling pathway) in the proximity of the TIR domain, initiating the intracellular events. Further downstream signals, involving other adaptor molecules and kinases,1 lead to local and systemic inflammatory responses and amplify innate immunity.5

The IL-1R ligands include IL-1α, IL-1β, IL-18, IL-33, IL-36α, IL-36β, and IL-36γ, which have agonistic functions, while few members (the two receptor antagonists IL-1Ra and IL-36Ra, and the cytokines IL-37 and IL-38) exhibit antagonistic activities.5 Based on their structural similarities, these cytokines are grouped into three subfamilies, as reported in Figure 1. In some of them, such as IL-1β and IL-1Ra proteins, a single replacement of an aromatic residue accounts for their agonistic and antagonistic activities, highlighting how structural plasticity may have broad implications for opposite effects.6 The reader is referred to2,7 for broader insights into the structural basis of IL-1 family members.

The anti-inflammatory IL-37, also termed IL-1F7, exerts a dual-anti-inflammatory function by acting both intracellularly, as a nuclear transcription factor and extracellularly, by initially binding IL-18Rα and then recruiting IL-1R8 (also named TIR-8 or SIGIRR), triggering its inhibitory signaling. Although IL-37 shares its dual functional activity with other cytokines of the IL-1 superfamily (namely IL-33), IL-37 is the unique member of this superfamily that regulates innate immune responses by inhibiting the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines. IL-37 has prominent protective functions in a number of inflammatory conditions, including allergy,8 atherosclerosis, spinal cord injury, obesity and metabolic disorders, bacterial and viral infections, autoimmune diseases, and different types of tumors.9 Considering the importance of inflammation in cancer onset and progression10 and the defensive role of immune cells and their mediators,11 the main idea of this review is to discuss the IL-37-mediated effects at the interface of immune and cancer cells in the tumor microenvironment (TME).

sec.title: IL-37: structure, gene, and splice variants
sec.p: The IL-37 gene, encoding a 17–25 kDa protein, is mapped to chromosome 2q12–13, in proximity to the regulatory regions of IL-1α and IL-1β. The transcript consists of six exons: exons 1 and 2, containing IL-37 caspase-1 cleavage sites, and exons 4–6, encoding functional proteins that are necessary for its β-trefoil structure.12,13 According to the encoded exons, five different splice variants (IL-37a–e), distinctly expressed in humans, have been identified, while no murine homolog has been reported so far.9,14 Among these variants, the IL-37a, IL-37b, and IL-37d isoforms encode molecules of biological interest, while IL-37c and IL-37e, are unlikely to generate functional products9,14,15 (Table 1). IL-37b, consisting of five of the six exons, is the longest, most characterized, and most abundant isoform. The caspase-1 cleavage site within IL-37 exon 1 is shared by all isoforms except for IL-37a, which instead possesses an elastase cleavage site within its unique exon 3 N-terminal sequence, but still exherts similar anti-inflammatory effects with IL-37b.16,17 Regarding IL-37b, the residues of D20 and E21 at the N-terminus of exon 1 (the first caspase-1 cleavage site) generate the mature form IL-37bΔ1-20 (aa 21–218), whereas the second site on exon 2 endengers a second mature form, named IL-37bΔ1-45 (aa 46–218), which is smaller but with stronger biological activity than the former.18,19 Short N-terminal peptides in the cytoplasm are also generated through caspase-1 cleavage.18,20,21

caption.p: Features of IL-37 isoforms. The transcript exons, the proten size and molecular weight, the binding to receptors, the function and the expression in different organs of the five splice variants of IL-37 are described.

fn.p: LNs: lymph nodes, BM: bone marrow, PBMC: peripheral blood mononuclear cells.

sec.p: The structural integrity of the caspase-1 cleavage site is crucial for both IL-37 processing and nuclear translocation, as single-residue mutations markedly reduce IL-37 active mature form and its localization into cell cytoplasm.18 Similar to IL-18, IL-37b transcript abundance is tightly regulated due to functional instability components within exon 5, which may lead to its rapid degradation.25

IL-37b is constitutively expressed in most of the human tissues, especially the testis, thymus, uterus, and peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMC)26 (Table 1). Among PBMCs, monocytes, and myeloid dendritic cells (mDCs) are the main sources of IL-37.25,27 This cytokine is also released by macrophages, T-cells,27 Treg-cells,28 natural killer (NK)-cells, and activated B-cells,29 as well as by tonsil plasma cells,12 keratinocytes,30 and tumor cell lines derived from epidermoid carcinoma, lung adenocarcinoma, and acute myelogenous leukemia (Table 1).26 Importantly, IL-37 has been reported to be in a unique monomer/dimer equilibrium: the monomers determine the cytokine extracellular effects, while high concentrations promote dimerization, which attenuates its function.31

sec.title: IL-37 production, maturation, and secretion
sec.p: Similar to IL-1β, IL-18, and IL-33,32 an immature form (pro-IL-37) is produced in the cytoplasm of several cell types. While IL-1β and IL-18 precursors are inactive, pro-IL-37 is active both in vitro and in vivo, and its maturation improves its biological activity.33 In these cells, the activation of sensors (in particular, NOD-like receptor family pyrin domain-containing 3 (NLRP3), nucleotide-binding domain, leucine-rich repeat-containing receptors (NLRs), absent in melanoma 2 (AIM2), and others, by DAMPs and PAMPs leads to the assembly of the inflammasome, an intracellular multiprotein complex that activates the effector caspase-1, which is responsible for the maturation/secretion of mature IL-37.14,34 Since inflammasome activity differs across cell types (i.e. macrophages and B cells), diverse IL-37 production and function in these cells may occur.

Importantly, the inflammasome can participate in tumorigenesis, apoptosis, immune evasion, and metastasis. Indeed, inflammasome components are upregulated in some tumors, and can be activated in cancer and other stromal cells by DAMPs, chemotherapy agents, and radiation, possibly explaining the variability of IL-37 in different tumors.35 Following cytosolic production, IL-37 is released via unconventional secretory pathways that bypass the conventional endoplasmic reticulum/Golgi trafficking route, such as secretory lysosome, microvesicle or exosome release, secretory autophagy, passive release during cell death.36,37 Recently, it has been shown that IL-37d inhibits NLRP3 inflammasome over-activation by regulating NLRP3 transcription through a signaling pathway mediated by IL-1R8, generating a potential interest on IL-37 in the treatment of inflammasome-dependent diseases.38 Several stimuli, including pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-18, IFN-γ, IL-1β, tumor necrosis factor alpha -TNF-α- and transforming growth factor β1 -TGF-β1), TLR ligands, and Phorbol-12-myristate-13-acetate (PMA),26 have been described to enhance IL-37 production, while type 2 cytokines, such as IL-10, IL-4 and granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF) have no impact on its expression.21 IL-37 mRNA is unstable under normal conditions and can be easily degraded. LPS (TLR4 ligand) and TGF-β1 enhance IL-37 mRNA stability, suggesting that this cytokine may not play a role in non-inflammatory or mild inflammatory states, whereas it is “called to action” under severe inflammatory conditions to regulate excessive immune responses.16 Finally, as previously mentioned, secreted IL-37 displays a negative feedback mechanism: it is more active at low concentrations (picomolar concentrations), while at higher concentrations, it dimerizes, forming poorly active or inactive molecules.31,39

sec.title: IL-37: intracellular and extracellular response
sec.p: IL-37 functions as an intracellular and extracellular cytokine14 (Figure 2); both of those signals may synergize and cooperate to elicit powerful anti-inflammatory activity.

Intracellular activity of IL-37. Inflammation triggers the cleavage of the IL-37 precursor into its mature forms (amino acids 21–218),9 which bind to phosphorylated Smad-3, a kinase downstream of the TGF-β1 receptor40 (Figure 2). The functional IL‐37/Smad-3 complex traffics into the nucleus, where it upregulates the expression of several non-receptor protein tyrosine phosphatase (PTPN) genes.40 This leads to inactivation of tyrosine phosphorylation-dependent signaling pathways such as p38, ERK, and JNK mitogen-activated protein kinases (MAPKs), PI3K, NF-κB, and STAT3.15 These, in turn, negatively modulate the transcription of TNF-α, IL-6, and other pro-inflammatory cytokine genes.9,17,41 In line with its nuclear activity, mutations preventing IL-37 nucleus translocation, in transgenic mice, resulted in the loss of cytokine suppressing function, both in vivo and in vitro.18 Nevertheless, since in these conditions the extracellular function of IL-37 was not compromised, the cytokine was still able to exert its protective activity.18,42 Recent studies have demonstrated novel biological functions for IL-37 isoforms by identifying previously unrecognized intracellular targets of IL-37 (beyond Smad-3), thereby regulating diverse signaling cascades.43 For instance, by binding to the two members of the GTPase family RAC119 and Rheb,44 and to the transcription factor CCAAT/enhancer binding protein beta (C/EBPβ),24 IL-37 inhibits numerous dynamic cellular processes45,46 including cell adhesion, cytoskeleton organization, invasion, differentiation, proliferation, metabolism, and survival.44-46 Overall, these data provide evidence that intracellular IL-37 regulates various processes by targeting multiple proteins, even though It is unclear whether these targets are common to all IL-37 isoforms. To understand some controversial activity of IL-37 in inflammation and cancer, further investigations on the regulation of individual IL-37 isoform/target interactions and its dependence on extracellular versus intracellular pathways are needed.
Extracellular activity of IL-37. As demonstrated in LPS-stimulated human PBMCs, IL-37 forms a tripartite complex with surface-expressed IL-18Rα and IL-1R847 (Figure 2).

The ternary IL-37/IL-18Rα/IL-1R8 complex promotes the polarization of monocyte-derived macrophages and DCs toward an anti-inflammatory/tolerant state by enhancing the downstream STAT3 signaling pathway, activating PTEN/FOXO/AMPK and MER/DOK/p62,48,49 and inhibiting MAPK, TAK-1/NF-kB, mTOR/FYN and PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling activities. Taken together, these effects suppress the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines and chemokines50,51 (Figure 2).

Interestingly, IL-37 exhibits a high structural similarity with the pro-inflammatory molecule IL-18, among other shared features.26 In this context, a computational study using cytokine preformed complexes revealed that the N-terminus (D3) of IL-37 faces the glycosylated N297 of the C-terminus of IL-18Ra, while IL-18 interacts with sequences of both N- (D1 and D2) and C-terminal (D3) lobe of the same receptor.22 In contrast to IL-37, IL-18 engages IL-18Rα and then recruits the IL-18Rβ chain thus assembling the IL-18Rα/IL-18/IL-18Rβ complex inducing a proinflammatory signal.52-54 Through binding with IL-18Rα,54 the two cytokines switch on/off crucial pathways of inflammation, with the affinity of IL-37 for IL-18Rα 50 times lower than IL-18, while the soluble IL-18BP can interfere with these binding dynamics thanks to its decoy activity55,56 (Figure 2). This activated receptor complex triggers both innate and adaptive immunity to produce IFN-γ and some type 2 cytokines in the presence of IL-12, IL-2 or IL-3.53 Similar to IL-37, each component of the IL-18Rα/IL-18/IL-18Rβ complex is crucial for IL-18-mediated signaling, as single receptor chain blockade prevents IL-18 binding and inhibits both pathway activation and signaling and, downstream, cytokine production.57-59

caption.p: The intracellular and extracellular activities of IL-37 lead to anti-inflammatory signals. Intracellular pathway: Following a pro-inflammatory stimulus, the production of intracellular IL-37 precursor is increased and caspase-1 is activated to cleave IL-37 precursor into its mature form. The complex formed by mature IL-37 and phosphorylated Smad-3 translocates into the nucleus, where it suppresses the transcription of pro-inflammatory genes. Extracellular pathway: IL-37 forms a tripartite complex with IL-18Rα and IL-1R8 to switch on the cell ant-iinflammatory machinery. In turn, IL‐37 activates GSK3β, which controls the abundance of IL‐1R8 on the cell surface by phosphorylation/ubiquitination/internalization/degradation mechanisms.

sec.title: IL-37 receptor and molecules interfering with its activity
IL-1R8 gene and protein expression patterns
sec.p: For a long time, the IL-1R8 complex has been considered an “orphan receptor”. Its gene, encoding ten exons and located on human chromosome 11, is translated into a protein of 410 amino acids. IL-1R8 displays a single extracellular Ig segment (different from the classical three Ig‐like domains of IL-1R members), a transmembrane region and an intracellular TIR domain followed by a unique long tail.60 Additionally, two conserved amino acid residues, essential for IL-1R signaling, have been replaced within the TIR region, rendering IL-1R8 a nonconventional member of the IL-1R family.60 Notably, the IL-1R8 extracellular domain displays several N-glycosylated sites, whose loss deeply impacts its localization to the cell surface and its function.61 In addition to this short isoform, which is abundantly expressed in human colon cancer,61 a long (IL-1R8L1) and a soluble form62 of IL-1R8 have been identified. IL-1R8L1 was detected in a number of human tissues and tumor cell lines, including chronic lymphocytic leukemia, T-cell leukemia and Burkitt's lymphoma.63 Despite not being functionally characterized, IL-1R8L1 has specific sub-membrane and cytoplasmic localization which might relate to intriguing intracellular activities.

IL-1R8, mostly expressed on lymphocytes (including T, B, and NK cells), monocytes, macrophages,64 and platelets,65 negatively regulates both IL-1R/TLR signaling pathways66 induced by some receptors as IL-1R1, IL-1R5, ST2, TLR1-4, and TLR7 and TLR9 engaged by PAMPS.67 IL-1R8 recruitment, upon IL-1 or LPS engagement, dampens TIR containing receptors and adaptor proteins-mediated signal transduction, leading, in turn, to inhibition of NF-kB and JNK pathways.68,69 As shown by many reports, IL-1R8 expression was downregulated under inflammatory conditions, following stimulation with TLR ligands, bacterial infections,70-74 on human leukemic B-lymphocytes,75 and colorectal carcinomas,61 as compared to normal cells and tissues.

sec.title: IL-1R8 signaling pathway
sec.p: As already mentioned, following stimulation with proinflammatory cytokines, IL-37b clusters with IL-18Rα and IL-1R8 on the surface of primary PBMCs, transfected RAW macrophages, HEK293 cells and A549 epithelial cells.47 In vitro and in vivo evidence highlights the requirement of such interaction for the anti-inflammatory signal of IL-37, as the knockdown of either IL-1R8 or IL-18Rα impairs such effect, upon inflammatory signals, through several mechanisms.47,76,77 In general, IL-1R8 is known to regulate some cellular pathways, as demonstrated by experiments performed by our group in which IL-1R8-silenced NK cells, either under basal conditions or upon activation, displayed changes in the expression of genes encoding multiple activating/inhibitory receptors, chemokine, and cytokine receptors and signaling transduction and transcription factors. In addition, changes were detected in the expression of genes regulating NK cell development and maturation or related to killing machinery and homing into the TME.78 In the presence of IL-37 alone or with IL-1R8, LPS-stimulated macrophages and DCs displayed expression changes in the expression of phosphorylated mediators involved in metabolism, NF-kB components, MAPK pathways and a number of multifunctional kinases, indicating the central role of this cytokine in multiple settings.47 The anti-inflammatory functions of IL-37 upon LPS challenge, involving factors such as tyrosine protein kinases Mer, PTEN, and STAT3,47 were also found to be upregulated in IL-1R8-silenced NK cells upon IL-15 stimulation78 (Figure 2).

sec.title: Molecules interfering with IL-37 activity
sec.p: A soluble form of IL-1R8 (sIL-1R8) has been described.62 Its levels are increased in HIV-infected individuals, inversely correlating with the surface receptor. In this system, sIL-1R8 was reported to function as a decoy molecule for IL-37, since it reduces the cytokine-mediated suppression of TNF-α release from LPS-stimulated human cells.62 As described for several surface molecules,79,80 IL-1R8 shedding into the circulation might be a key event in the regulation of the receptor expression, offering a valuable opportunity to modulate ligand-induced activation processes. Similar IL-1R8-driven signaling events have been reported for the IL-33-ST2-IL-1R8 complex upon binding of IL-33 to its receptor ST2,66 suggesting that IL-1R8 may also suppress type 2-associated immune responses. Moreover, IL-37 was demonstrated to physically interact with IL-18BP and to potentiate IL-18BP-mediated neutralization of IL-18 secretion in human NK cells.54 Consistent with previous findings,81 the existence of the IL-18BP-IL-37-IL-18Rβ complex has been postulated54 (Figure 2). In this context, removal of IL-18Rβ would prevent the assembly of the IL-18 receptor (IL-18Rβ-IL-18Rα), thus affecting IL-18-mediated activities and regulating downstream signaling.54 Regulation of pro- and anti-inflammatory signaling, as well as maintenance of an appropriate balance, is crucial at different levels, as revealed by the involvement of multiple players. Among them, it is likely that the release of the IL-37 precursor and its mature form, as well as their extracellular function,77 represent a delayed negative feedback response to immune activation, and that IL-37-IL-18 interactions are tightly coordinated. In addition, the existence of a truncated form of IL-37 (following amino-terminal proteolysis catalyzed by neutrophil elastase or cathepsin S), exhibiting pro-inflammatory activity by binding to the IL-36 receptor, has also been postulated,82 further underscoring its complex and multifaceted biological roles in physiological conditions.82 In agreement with this concept, the prevalence of either truncated or full-length IL-37 or IL-18, IL-18R or IL-18BP, possibly neutralizing IL-18-mediated effects,55 might differentially impact pathological processes, further supporting the importance of cytokine levels and their interactions in maintaining physiological functions. The molecules and molecular complexes interfering with IL-37 activity are summarized in Table 2.

caption.p: Molecules and molecule complexes interfering with IL-37 activity.

tr.td: IL-18Rβ subtraction potentiates IL-18BP-mediated IL-18 inhibition

sec.title: Cytokine activity in cancer cells
sec.p: IL-37 involvement has been associated with either good or poor outcomes in multiple cancer types. In particular, IL-37 negatively regulates cancer initiation, growth and spread83-87 in various tumors, except for ovarian cancer.88,89

Indeed, data obtained in murine models of fibrosarcoma and hepatic cell carcinoma (HCC) showed that intratumoral injection of an adenovirus or a cell line expressing the IL-37 gene suppressed or delayed tumor growth compared to control mice.90,91 Importantly, although IL-37 production by tumor cells or its serum levels are increased in breast, ovarian cancers and oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC),12,89,92 they are usually downregulated in non-small-cell lung carcinoma (NSCLC), renal cell carcinoma (RCC), colon-rectal cancer (CRC), multiple myeloma (MM) and HCC,90,93-96 with cytokine serum levels being significantly lower than in healthy controls. Hereafter, such downregulation in cancer patients could represent a mechanism by which cancer cells evade the anti-tumor activity of IL-37. Accordingly, elevated levels of IL-37, among bladder urothelial carcinoma (BLCA) and HCC patients were reported to correlate with better overall survival, in contrast to its diminished expression in the advanced stages of cancer progression.90,97 Conversely, other studies have reported unfavorable functions of IL-37,89,98 as its levels correlated with increased tumor growth, spreading, and recurrence.88,89 In view of these opposite profiles, low levels of IL-37, but enhanced expression of IL-18, were simultaneously described in glioblastoma, lung cancer, breast cancer, HCC, and leukemia, further highlighting the complex interplay between these two cytokines and their decoy molecules.55 The mechanisms of the opposite effects of IL-37, which are observed in various tumors, will be discussed aside (section 5). IL-37 can influence tumor growth and progression through a number of intracellular mechanisms, acting either directly or indirectly on tumor or TME cells. IL-37 directly impacts the proliferation, invasiveness, and metastasis of tumor cells by: (i) inhibiting paxillin/FAK pathways (migration and adhesion), (ii) regulating IL-6, IL-8, and MMP2/9 (tumor cell invasiveness and viability), and (iii) inhibiting STAT3, Runt-related transcription factor (RUNX2), and β-catenin (proliferation, invasion, and TME inflammation).95,99-101

IL‐37 can indirectly inhibit tumor cell growth and induce apoptosis in most tumors102 by: (i) regulating STAT3 expression and activation that favoring the shift from M2 to tumoricidal M1 macrophage phenotype, (ii) changing Smad-3 phosphorylation isoforms from oncogenic to a suppressive signaling, and (iii) inhibiting MAPK (ERK/JNK/p38) phosphorylation, which is overactivated in cancer, thereby reducing proliferation, angiogenesis, and immune suppression, (iv) inhibiting the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway, which improves autophagy (favoring autophagosome formation) and reduces tumor growth and cell survival, (v) impairing RAC1 signaling, which increases the methylation of N6 methyl adenosine RNA (the RNA modification critical for tumorigenesis) suppressing tumor cell proliferation, and (vi) upregulating the expression of Bim, a pro-apoptotic molecule, promoting apoptosis.17,19,90,103-107 Among the multiple pathways described, it is likely that, to obtain its anti-tumor effects, IL-37 exploits each tumor-specific mechanism, mostly related to the different carcinogenesis pathways involved. Furthermore, IL-37 plays a role in regulating tumoral angiogenesis by decreasing the expression of pro-angiogenic factors and favoring the anti-angiogenic expression pattern.108 M2-type tumor associated macrophages (TAMs), as well as tumor cells, can produce pro-angiogenic molecules (VEGF, MMP2/7/9/12, and Cox2), whose release is inhibited in vitro by IL-37.108 Notably, hypoxic conditions in the TME potentiate the anti-angiogenic role of IL-37.108 In conclusion, even though the anti-inflammatory properties of IL-37 plays a major regulatory role in several tumors, the complex IL-37 network can suppress tumor growth, progression, and invasiveness through multiple pathways, providing useful information for prognostic value, medication regimens, and targeted immunotherapy.

sec.title: Cytokine activity on TME cells
sec.p: A large body of research highlights the involvement of both innate and adaptive immunity in tumor development and progression.109 Inflammatory immune cells within the TME act either as immunosurveillance effectors, capable of impairing cancer growth, or, conversely, as direct or indirect promoters of cancer progression by delivering pro-tumorigenic signals or by participating in the shaping of an immunosuppressive milieu.110 Cytokines and growth factors with activating, suppressing or differentiating activities are among the key drivers of TME cell plasticity.110 The described pro- and anti-tumor activities of IL-37 on macrophages, DCs, T, B, NK cells, neutrophils, as well as myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs) in the TME are reviewed below (Figure 3).

caption.p: Specific activity of IL-37 on immune cells in the TME. Following pre-IL-37 cleavage, mature IL-37 differently modulates immune cell functions within TME: in particular, IL-37 favors anti-tumor immune response by blocking BM-neutrophils and MDSC recruitment and function, potentiating DC maturation, shifting M2- into M1-type phagocytes, and improving NK and T-cell activity. However, in some circumstances, IL-37 may favor tumor progression improving Treg cell proliferation and T/NK dysfunction or exhaustion.

sec.title: IL-37 activity on monocytes-macrophages and dendritic cells
sec.p: Myeloid cells, including monocytes, DCs, and macrophages are crucial components of the TME that, in their persistently activated phenotype, usually support tumor growth.111 For instance, in cervical cancer, IL-37 inhibited the expression of the “don't eat me” tumor molecule CD47, thus enhancing macrophage-mediated recognition via signal regulatory protein-alpha (SIRPα) and phagocytosis of cancer cells. In this setting, the combination of recombinant human (rh)IL-37 and CD47 monoclonal antibody (mAb) inhibited cervical cancer progression in vivo, more efficiently compared to monotherapy, offering encouraging insights on how this cytokine might potentially improve CD47 immune-related checkpoint anticancer therapy and reduce immune evasion mechanisms.83 Zhang et al. showed that HCC patient-derived PBMCs were of M2-type and exhibited decreased IL-37 expression.112 In vitro experiments indicated that, upon IL-37 transfection, TAMs from HCC patients polarized into M1 type.112 This shift led to an increased cytotoxicity and production of M1-related cytokines IL-12, TNF-α and IL-1β, supporting the anti-tumour role of IL-37 while, IL-37 knockdown promoted tumor proliferation, migration, and invasion in human hepatoma cell lines.112 In agreement, in an HCC xenograft mouse model, the inoculation of IL-37-transfected TAMs into recipients resulted in a substantial reduction in tumour growth, compared to control mice.112 The M2 to M1 shift, as well as the functional inhibition of tumor cell lines, is probably due to the inhibition of the IL-6/STAT3 signaling,112 as further confirmed by data demonstrating that IL-37 inhibited NSCLS and RCC growth94,100 through M2 to M1 switch, by suppressing the same pathways. In another study, IL-37 overexpression in HCC resulted in DC recruitment in vitro and in vivo and improved anti-tumor T cell effects.113 Overall, these results support the notion that IL-37, even though decreased, plays a hepatoprotective role during HCC development by influencing the immune response and promoting an anti-cancer environment.

Notably, in non-neoplastic inflammatory conditions, IL-37 does not promote M1 polarization, suggesting that the inflamed environment plays a key role in the effects of such cytokines.77 As observed in experimental inflammatory models,38,114 IL-37 suppressed M1 polarization through inhibition of the Notch1, NF-kB,115 and NLRP3116 signaling pathways, thereby impairing the inflammatory response. The different activity of IL-37 on healthy (suppression) and some neoplastic (promotion) macrophages to M1 polarization raises the question of whether IL-37 functions in opposite ways in distinct environments, and in the presence of certain stimuli or proteins/pathways involved.43 While under inflammatory conditions, IL-37 promotes both macrophage- and DC-mediated anti-inflammatory states,38,114,116,117 its effects on TAMs in the TME are more complicated. The anti-tumor effect of IL-37 is usually related to macrophage M1 differentiation and enhanced phagocytosis via the suppression of the STAT3 signaling cascade83,112 and DC activation, which promotes T cell-mediated anti-tumor responses. Nevertheless, different mechanisms associating IL-37 activity to reduce the maturation and function of CD103+ DCs and enhanced tumor burden have been described in DMBA/TPA skin cancer, thus undelying the contrasting contribution of IL-37 to antitumor responses in different TMEs118 (Figure 3). Furthermore, it is likely that, in various tumors, in addition to inflammation, the protective effect of IL-37-driven macrophages likely depends on the tumor type and stage, as well as on the IL-37 level and isoform (or its decoy molecules) within the TME compartment. Differences in IL-37 activity in different tumors could be due to TAMs expressing differential levels of IL-18Ra and IL-1R8 (or secreting various levels of decoy molecules, such as IL-18BP and soluble IL-1R8), which mediate opposite effects. Since IL-37 works at very low concentrations, one of the limiting factors may be related to the variable level of IL-1R8 expression among different species and cell types.119 Indeed, M1 cells express more surface and total IL-1R8 compared to M2 macrophages, accounting for the lower responsiveness of these latter cells.77 The complex IL-37 targeting, at least in some tumors, could offer a powerful weapon to restore homeostatic conditions and potentiate anti-tumor activity. Accordingly, further studies in multiple types of cancer are needed to better define the different IL-37-mediated mechanisms.

sec.title: IL-37 activity on NK cells
sec.p: NK cells are involved in first-line defense against tumors and have been characterized among the immune cells exhibiting the highest levels of IL-1R8.64 Data obtained from tissues have shown that high levels of IL-37 were associated with elevated numbers of “adaptive” CD57+NK cells and with better overall and disease-free survival. Indeed, IL-37 overexpression suppressed the progression of HCC in vivo by increasing CD57+ NK cell infiltration into tumor tissue, but the mechanisms have not been fully clarified.120 Conversely, in a model of murine fibrosarcoma, the anti-tumor role of adenovirus-mediated IL-37 treatment was abolished by the lack of the NK-associated cytokines IFN-γ, IL-12 and the IL-18-induced Fas ligand, depicting an interesting mixture of IL-37, IL-12, and IL-18-mediated pathways in anti-tumor immunity.91

It is well known that Treg cells suppress the proliferation, IFN-γ production, degranulation, and cytotoxicity of NK cells. Treg-mediated suppression was associated with the downregulation of TIM3, and upregulation of NK-cell inhibitory receptors such as PD-1 and IL-1R8. Importantly, IL-37 abrogated Treg-mediated suppression of canonical NK cells, whereas adaptive CD57+ NK cells were resistant under the same conditions.121 In this context, treatment with rIL-37 and IL-15 enhanced NK cell cytotoxicity against multiple human cell lines as well as cytokine secretion. This improvement in NK cell anti-tumor functions was correlated with IL-1R8 degradation processes via IL-37-induced phosphorylation of glycogen synthase kinase 3β,122 similar to previous results in lung epithelial cells.123 The relevant role of IL-1R8 degradation mechanisms has been confirmed by data showing that, upon IL-15 stimulation, IL-37 treatment of IL-1R8-silenced NK cells results in similar effector functions. This further confirms that, removing the IL-1R8 brake, either by specific silencing, as previously described,78 or by treatment with IL-37 in a cytokine-driven milieu, NK cell anti-tumor activities are equally potentiated.

sec.title: IL-37 activity on neutrophils and myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs)
sec.p: A major interest has recently been focused on the heterogeneity of granulocytic populations within TME, undergoing a series of tumor-driven transformations, enhancing their spontaneous migration from the bone marrow (BM)124,125 and leading to a differentiated cancer-promoting phenotype.126 In several cancer types, the presence of MDSCs is associated with poor prognosis and a limited response to therapy. In different murine models of cancer, IL-37 expression is correlated with reduced recruitment of BM neutrophils and MDSCs.24,84,127 This is in line with enhanced levels of CXCL8 (the selective neutrophil chemokine) upon IL-37 reduction in colon epithelial cells128 and elevated counts of CD66b+ tumor-associated neutrophils (TAN) in CRC associated with low IL‐37 expression.129 Mechanistically, IL-37 inhibited neutrophil C/EBPβ-mediated ATP generation processes, preventing BM neutrophils migration.24 This effect was related to deep MDSC metabolic reprogramming impairing MDSC immunosuppressive effects that allowed increased T cell anti-tumor immunity.127 IL-37d, which is highly similar to the IL-37b isoform, suppressed early-stage neutrophil infiltration into the lung by downregulating the expression levels of S100A8/A9 chemoattractants, MMP9, and TLR3 in lung epithelial cells.84 Based on these studies, strategies promoting IL-37-mediated suppression of BM neutrophils in early stage of cancers might be useful for removing one of the key suppressor cells, favoring tumor progression and metastasis. Studies in this direction are needed.

sec.title: IL-37 activity on T cells
sec.p: Among the key components of the TME, CD4+ and CD8+ cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTLs) exert strong anti-tumor activity, by suppressing blood vessel formation and directly lysing target cells via perforin/granzyme/FAS-related pathways.130,131 IL-37 can regulate the immune system, promoting adaptive T-cell immunity. In vitro study indicated that IL-37 could directly inhibit the chemotaxis of Treg cells in a lung carcinoma cell line, thus favoring T effector cells.132 In the model of murine fibrosarcoma previously reported, the adenovirus-mediated IL-37 treatment protected hosts against fibrosarcoma rechallenge, suggesting a response due to memory T cells.91 In an experimental breast cancer model, the anti-tumor activity of IL-37 was abolished in nude and SCID mice, suggesting the involvement of cellular adaptive immunity.133 In murine HCC models, IL-37 was associated with increased numbers of activated CD4+ and CD8+ T cells and higher cytotoxic activity of tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) against tumor cells.127 IL-37 also promotes CD4+ T cell proliferation in vitro, indicating a direct involvement in anti-tumor T cell response.133 Few reports have been focused on the mechanisms involved in IL-37 activity on T-cells within TME. There is a general consensus that IL-37 may inhibit pro-tumor inflammation which favors TME suppression, thereby unlocking the adaptive immune response. Similarly, the enhancement of suppressing activity of Treg cells induced by IL-37 could quench TME hyperinflammation favoring tumor progression.28,113 Another interesting mechanism has been reported in a study on aging, showing that the treatment of aged mice with rIL-37 resulted in improved T cell function together with a youthful restoration of PDCD1, LAT, and STAT4 gene expression levels in aged CD4+ T cells and LAT in aged CD8+ T cells.134 Furthermore, rIL-37 downregulated TNF-α-induced NFkB and inhibited the surface expression of PD-1 and TIM3 on effector CD4+ and CD8+ T cells.134 Considering that aging-related T cell dysfunction is associated with increased levels of these two checkpoint molecules (involved in pro-tumor activity),135,136 these data suggest that IL-37 mediates the “rejuvenation” of aged T-cells. In agreement, IL-37 treatment also improved the efficacy of aged chimeric antigen receptor (CAR)-T cells and extended the survival of mice transplanted with leukemia cells.134 In contrast to the beneficial effect of infiltrating CD4+ and CD8+ CTLs in most cancers,137,138 a number of TME stimuli have been recently described to shape T cell profiles, leading to their “functional exhaustion”.131,139-141 These dysfunctions, accompanied by the upregulated expression of several surface inhibitory receptors131 favor the concept that they may favor tumor escape. By using a CRC model, IL-37 was reported to limit IL-12/IL-18-mediated CD8+ T cells cytotoxic activity and promote T cell dysfunction via IL-1R8 pathway.142 While IL-18 signaling with IL-12, is acknowledged to boost CTL-mediated activation,143,144 IL-37 interaction with IL-1R8-expressing CTL is likely to antagonize such effects thus favouring their pro-tumor immunity142 instead of unleashing cancer-fighting cells.

sec.title: Mechanisms of pro- and anti-tumor activity of IL-37
sec.p: There is a general consensus that IL-37 displays a robust anti-tumor activity in the great majority of in vitro and in vivo models exploiting various mechanisms,91,93,94,103,120,133,145 as the suppression of IL-6/STAT-3 signaling pathway in NSCLC and of β-catenin in CRCs.95,100

However, in some human neoplasia, IL-37 can exert a pro-tumor activity (Table 3). For instance, in ovarian cancer, IL-37 levels directly correlate with poor prognosis, since it generates an escape mechanism by producing a highly suppressive environment, i.e., an increase in IL-10 + DC plus IL-37-neg and IL-37-pos Treg cells leads to the inhibition of tumor-specific T-cell priming/proliferation.28,146 Moreover, in brain tumors, the simultaneous increase in IL-37 and IL-18BP serum levels, which are strictly positively correlated, dampens anti-tumor immune response toward tumor progression; in this case, IL-37 is highly expressed by Treg cells, and its levels are positively associated with Foxp3 expression and immunosuppressive function.28,147 Last, in tumors with a sustained immune response such as melanoma, excess of IL-37 can downregulate both innate and adaptive immunity, modulating consequently anti-tumor responses.148 Moreover, despite its anti-angiogenic activity, a pro-angiogenic function of IL-37 has been documented in both physiological and pathological settings, particularly upon hypoxic conditions.98 Importantly, since the IL-37-mediated secretion and proliferation of endothelial cells is not influenced by neutralizing IL‐1R8 or IL‐18Rα, this finding has led the hypothesis of the existence of an unknown receptor of IL-37. It has been identified with ALK1 receptor complex in which TGF‐β may act as the bridging molecule mediating IL‐37 binding to the TGF‐β receptor complex.149 However, even though some in vitro reports indicate that IL-37 can exert pro-angiogenic activity on HUVEC cell lines, the supernatants from IL-37-expressing tumor cells shift its activity to anti-angiogenesis, indicating a balance of factors produced by tumor cells in which IL-37 plays a clear anti-angiogenic role.150

caption.p: Pro- and anti-tumor activity of IL-37 on TME- and tumor cells.

tr.th: Effects on function of TME cells

Increased maturation of DC, their processing of TA and T cell activation
Secretion of IL-2, IL-12, IL-12p70, IFN-α and IFN-γ
Reduced TIM-3 expression on canonical NK cells

Limited IL-12/IL-18-induced functions of CD8 + T cells via IL-1R8
Enhanced CTLA-4 and FOXP3/IL-10 production/Suppressed T cell activation/proliferation

Induction of autophagy/apoptosis in cancer cells

Promoted the conversion of pSmad3 signaling from JNK/pSmad3L/c-Myc oncogenic signaling to pSmad3C/P21 tumor-suppressive signaling
Impaired tumor cell cell migration, invasion, proliferation, colony formation and cancer stem cells
Reduced tumor cell proliferation, invasion, and metastasis

fn.p: Abbreviations: Bone marrow (BM), Matrix-metallo peptidase (MMP) 2/9, N6-methyladenosine (m6A); phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (PI3K)/protein kinase B/ mammalian target of rapamycin Complex 2 (PI3K/AKT/mTor), Programmed cell death protein 1 (PD1), Ras-related C3 botulinum toxin substrate 1 (Rac1), Runt-related transcription factor 2 (RUNX2), Signal transducer and activator of transcription (STAT)3/4, T cell immunoglobulin and mucin domain containing-3 (TIM3), Tyrosine-protein kinase (Tyrk) 2.

sec.p: Some mechanisms have been proposed to explain the controversial activity of IL-37 in inflammation and cancer, which are prevalently related to feedback pathways to downregulate the cytokine excess.

A first mechanism, as previously reported, is related to the concentration of molecules/molecular complexes (depicted in Table 2) in different cells, tissues, and tumors, which may be responsible for the variable response to IL-37. Indeed, the limiting factor of the IL-37 response, which acts at very low concentrations, is likely due to the expression of these molecules, which is highly variable in different species and cell types (IL-1R8 being more expressed in M1 than in M2 cells).77

A second mechanism, modulating IL-37 levels/activity is related to cytokine proteolysis catalyzed by environmental enzymes (such as neutrophil elastase or cathepsin S): this truncated form of IL-37 (spanning from positions 53–218) engages IL-36R, leading to pro-inflammatory activity. The identification of the pro-inflammatory activity associated with shortened IL-37 underscores the complexity and multifaceted biological roles of this molecule in several pathophysiological conditions.82 Two additional major regulatory mechanisms of IL-37 activity are represented by IL-1R8 degradation123 and IL-37 dimerization, which strongly inhibits its anti-inflammatory capacity.39

The IL-37-induced phosphorylation of glycogen synthase kinase 3β (GSK3β) leads to IL-1R8 degradation in NK and epithelial cells.122,123 Residues 51–101 in GSK3β have been identified as the IL-1R8 binding domain. IL-37 activation of GSK3β promotes receptor phosphorylation, IL-1R8 cell internalization, and degradation through disruption of the association with the deubiquitinase USP13.123

The dimerization of IL-37 monomers at high concentrations may represent a physiological negative feedback loop to avoid excessive activity of this molecule. If this “switch-off mechanism” is operative is relevant to establish the cut-off dose to induce dimerization in both in vitro and in vivo models, the dimerization dose in different cells/tissues or, in the same tissue, upon different types of inflammation. Furthermore, this mechanism could limit the potential therapeutic use of IL-37 in tumors and chronic inflammatory diseases. It has been shown that mutations at the dimer interface (IL-37D73K and IL-37Y85A) specifically destroy dimer formation, giving stable monomers displaying greater anti-inflammatory properties compared with native IL-37.31

Finally, an important variable influencing different IL-37 effects consists of the activity of IL-37 isoforms other than IL-37b, such as IL-37a and IL-37d.24 Recent data have provided evidence that intracellular IL-37 (especially IL-37d) regulates various processes by targeting multiple proteins (beyond Smad3), such as RAC1, Rheb and C/EBPb.43 It has been speculated that the selective intracellular IL-37/target interactions and the dependence on its extracellular versus intracellular pathways may be environment-dependent. Indeed, the post-translational modifications or conformational changes of IL-37, as well as specific cofactors, could favor the selective binding of IL-37 to its targets. Such cofactors could influence the affinity and specificity of IL-37 interactions, thereby determining its functional outcomes in different cellular environments. In this context, the study of IL-37 related to the intrinsic environmental features of each tumor as histo-types, differentiation of cancer cells, degree of suppression in the TME, and involvement of the anti-tumor immune response, is mandatory.86

In conclusion, IL-37 exerts a clear-cut inhibitory function on tumor progression, invasiveness, and metastasis in many in vitro and in vivo models (Table 3). Some well-documented pro-tumor effects of IL-37 can be ascribed to some activities overexpressed inside the complex network of signals (and mechanisms) up- or downregulating cytokine levels, which are strictly related to the environment of a specific tumor.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: For many decades, chronic inflammation has been considered one of the main mechanisms facilitating cancer onset and progression. Indeed, immune and non-immune multi-directional crosstalk within the cancer-stromal cell niche is likely to promote tumor growth or break the tolerance to it.

The IL-1 family member IL-37, triggered by several pro-inflammatory stimuli, is a potent suppressor of inflammation and innate immunity, thus gaining recent attention in the context of cancer development.9
While the impact of IL-37 has been extensively described in inflammatory conditions by exploiting mouse models, the effects of IL-37 on immune cells against human tumors have been less explored.

Expanding our knowledge of cancer surroundings has led to a “TME-centric” perspective, consisting of numerous communicating specialized microenvironments within the TME, the immune cells and their released mediators playing a major role.152 More recently, CAFs stimulating TAMs through their released mediators were placed at the top of a hierarchical network of interactions among cell types,153 stressing the crucial need to further explore cell communication patterns.

The majority of reports indicate that IL-37 suppresses tumor progression through multiple pathways, including mediating immunoregulation in the TME, reducing tumor cell proliferation, controlling angiogenesis, as well as inhibiting cancer invasion and metastasis. The rare pro-tumor effects of IL-37 can be ascribed to some activity overexpressed inside the complex network modulating cytokine levels, which are closely related to the environment of a specific tumor. Extensive studies are needed to establish the exact role of IL-37 in various tumor contexts and the underlying mechanisms. In this context, the lack of an IL-37 murine homologue limits in vivo studies; thus, all reported data rely on transgenic murine strains expressing human IL-37.

If IL-37 is confirmed as a pivotal player at the interface between tumor and immune cells, definitively showing its anti-inflammatory and anti-tumor activity, new strategies for an effective anti-cancer immunotherapy targeting the network of this molecule will be available.
